explanation_md: `a::val` resolves to the `val` inside namespace `a` (value 1) and `b::val` resolves to the `val` inside namespace `b` (value 2). The two names do not collide because they live in different namespaces. Output is `1 2`.

prompt_md:
What does `::` mean when used as a prefix with nothing on its left side (e.g., `::pi`)?

- a. It refers to the global namespace scope.
- b. It refers to the most recently opened namespace.
- c. It is shorthand for `std::`.
- d. It resolves a namespace named after the current file.

options.a: It refers to the global namespace scope.
options.b: It refers to the most recently opened namespace.
options.d: It resolves a namespace named after the current file.

explanation_md: When `::` has no left-hand operand, it is the global scope resolution operator — it looks for the name at the top level of the program, outside any namespace. It has nothing to do with the most recently opened namespace or with `std::`.

options.a: `double_val` is a reserved keyword in C++ and cannot be used as a function name.
options.b: The function is inside namespace `util` but called without the `util::` qualifier.
options.c: `n * 2` should be written as `n + n` to avoid a type error.
options.d: `std::cout` cannot print the result of a function call directly.

explanation_md: Names defined inside a namespace are not visible in the global scope. `double_val(4)` must be written as `util::double_val(4)` to resolve the name correctly. Without the qualifier the compiler reports that `double_val` was not declared.
Both `say` and `say2` are defined inside namespace `greet` (across two separate blocks, which is valid — the compiler merges them). Calling both in sequence prints `Hi there` followed by a newline.

prompt_md:
Inside a namespace block, a function refers to another function in the SAME namespace. Does it need the `namespace::` qualifier?

- a. Yes, always — you must always use the full qualified name.
- b. No — names in the same namespace are visible to each other without a qualifier.
- c. Only when the second function is defined later in the file.
- d. Only if the second function has parameters.

options.a: Yes, always — you must always use the full qualified name.
options.b: No — names in the same namespace are visible to each other without a qualifier.
options.c: Only when the second function is defined later in the file.
options.d: Only if the second function has parameters.

explanation_md: Inside a namespace, all members of that same namespace are in scope without qualification. You only need `namespace::name` when accessing the member from outside the namespace. The qualifier is not required (or even standard style) for intra-namespace calls.
